statement: Shadow DOM content extraction failure: web components using Shadow DOM encapsulate their HTML in a shadow root that is inaccessible via standard `document.querySelector()` calls from outside the shadow host. Modern web components (Lit, Stencil, Polymer) and some UI libraries (Ionic, Shoelace) use Shadow DOM extensively. Failure: `page.query_selector('.product-price')` returns null even though the element is visually present because it's inside a shadow root. Playwright's solution: `page.locator('css=.host-element >> css=.product-price')` — the `>>` combinator pierces shadow DOM boundaries. Alternatively: `await page.evaluate('document.querySelector(".host-element").shadowRoot.querySelector(".product-price").textContent')`. For deeply nested shadow DOMs: chain multiple `shadowRoot` accesses in the evaluate call. Scrapy and aiohttp+BeautifulSoup cannot access shadow DOM content — requires browser automation. Detection signal: `page.query_selector()` returns null but element is visible in browser DevTools — check if element is inside a shadow root in the Elements panel.

statement: Web content change monitoring (competitor monitoring, price tracking) pattern: scheduled extraction + change detection + notification pipeline. (1) Schedule: `APScheduler` cron job per target URL with domain-appropriate frequency; (2) Extraction: fetch current content, store with timestamp and SHA-256 hash; (3) Diff detection: compare current content hash with previous — if different, compute semantic diff using `difflib.unified_diff()` for text; (4) Relevance filtering: not all changes are relevant — parse structured fields (price, availability, headline) specifically; alert only when monitored fields change; (5) Alert routing: minor changes (typo fixes) → daily digest email; price changes > 10% → immediate Slack notification; page removal (404) → PagerDuty; (6) History retention: store full content history for 90 days for retrospective analysis. Technology stack: APScheduler + PostgreSQL for state + Slack webhook for alerts. UNSAID: monitoring more than 10,000 URLs requires a dedicated scheduling system (Celery Beat + Redis) rather than APScheduler in-process.

statement: Playwright's async API has a global event loop constraint in Python: all Playwright async operations must run within the same event loop, and the event loop must be created before any Playwright objects. The constraint: creating a `Playwright` instance in one thread and using it in another raises `RuntimeError: no current event loop in thread`. For FastAPI integration (which manages its own event loop per request): initialize Playwright in the application startup event handler and store the instance as a module-level variable, not per-request. Do not use `asyncio.run()` inside async functions — it creates a new event loop which conflicts with the existing one. For sync code that needs async Playwright: use `asyncio.new_event_loop()` in a dedicated thread. Playwright's `sync_playwright()` context manager creates a synchronous wrapper around the async API using a background thread — safe for sync code but cannot be nested inside `async def` functions without `asyncio.to_thread`.

statement: The decision to store raw HTML vs. extracted text vs. embeddings for a web content corpus depends on downstream use cases and storage economics. Raw HTML (100-500KB/page): enables re-extraction when algorithms improve, supports JavaScript-rendered content archaeology, preserves all metadata. Cost: 100,000 pages × 300KB = 30GB, ~$0.69/month on S3. Extracted text (1-10KB/page): fast to query, direct LLM input, language-agnostic processing. Cost: 100,000 pages × 5KB = 500MB, negligible. Embeddings (3-12KB/page for 1536-dim float32): enables semantic search without re-computation. Cost: 100,000 pages × 6KB = 600MB for pgvector. Recommendation: store all three levels with raw HTML compressed in cold storage, extracted text in Postgres, and embeddings in pgvector. The raw HTML cold storage investment pays off every time extraction algorithms improve — re-extraction from raw HTML is free compared to re-crawling. Delete raw HTML only when you're confident the extraction is complete and algorithms are stable.
